levels.body: “Sweet” in this context is synonymous with “pleas[ant]” (“sweet, *adj*. and *adv*.” 5). “Sickly” can be explained with being “[t]horoughly tired or weary of a thing”, in this case of supposedly pleasant monotony (“sick *adj*. and *n*.” 5.a.).
worksCited: “sick, *adj*. and *n*.” *OED Online*, Oxford University Press, March 2022, www.oed.com/view/Entry/179187. Accessed 15 April 2022.
“sweet”, *adj*. and *adv*.” *OED Online*, Oxford University Press, March 2022, www. oed.com/view/Entry/195665. Accessed 15 April 2022.

levels.body: The speaker frequently refers to herself as being stuck in a state of monotony: the landscape of Circe’s island is described as unchangeable (see lines 33-47), and Circe’s own life is presented as dull and uneventful (see lines 58-65).

The combination of contradictory words preceding “monotony” emphasises Circe’s rejection of a sweet monotonous life, since this sweetness is exhausting and irritating her. 
The speaker in “Circe” longs to break the “sickly sweet monotony” of her stagnant life and to subject herself to and enjoy the passions that a storm, may bring: see lines 58-65. This yearning for action is highlighted by Circe’s description of the island’s unchanging cycle of life (cf. [[storm]]). 
The state of monotony can also be understood to describe Circe’s uneventful love life. The storm, then as a metaphor, stands for the passions that she hopes the ship-wrecked sailors can incite within her.

In the context of mid-Victorian debates about the role of (especially middle-class) women in society, Circe’s desire to break the monotony of her life may be an echo of the voices of women in Webster’s time that were demanding more emotional and mental stimulation (Fiske 486). Indeed, Circe’s claim for action in her life (see line 65) resonates with Nightingale’s “Cassandra”, an influential text written in 1852 that advocates for women’s right to exert their passions, and with which Webster was probably familiar (Fiske 486). 
Circe’s implied sexual liberation touches upon another sensitive issue in an era in which women’s sexuality was not discussed openly (Sutphin 376).
worksCited: Fiske, Shanyn. “Augusta Webster and the Social History of Myth.” *Women’s Studies*, vol. 40, no. 4, Routledge, 2011, pp. 469-490. doi.org/10.1080/00497878.2011.561745.
Nightingale, Florence. “Cassandra.” *Cassandra and Other Selections from Suggestions for Thought*, edited by Mary Poovey, Pickering & Chatto, 1991, pp. 205-231.
Sutphin, Christine. “The Representation of Women’s Heterosexual Desire in Augusta Webster’s ‘Circe’ and ‘Medea in Athens’.” *Women’s Writing*, vol. 5, no. 3, 1998, pp.  373-393.
